title: Rotavirus deaths prevented by vaccination
subtitle: The number of [rotavirus](#dod:rotavirus) deaths of children under five years old that were prevented in 2016 due to the rotavirus vaccine.
caption:
Rotavirus deaths prevented by vaccination

The number of rotavirus deaths of children under five years old that were prevented in 2016 due to the rotavirus vaccine.

This choropleth map displays the estimated number of rotavirus deaths prevented among children under five years old in 2016, utilizing a color scale that ranges from pale yellow for zero prevented deaths to dark blue for values reaching 3,000. The visualization reveals a distinct geographic concentration of high impact across sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia, where the darkest blue shading indicates the largest absolute numbers of averted fatalities. Nigeria, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and Ethiopia appear in the highest category, falling within the 1,000 to 3,000 range, signifying that vaccination efforts in these nations prevented thousands of deaths. India and China also display deep blue tones, placing them in the upper bands of the scale alongside several West African nations such as Niger and Mali. In contrast, the map shows light green and pale yellow shading across most of Europe, North America, and Australia, indicating that the number of deaths prevented in these regions falls below 100 or near zero. South American countries like Brazil and Peru show intermediate values in the teal range, generally between 100 and 300 prevented deaths. Large portions of the map, including Greenland and parts of the Middle East, are marked with hatching to denote areas where no data is available. The distribution of color intensity highlights that the burden of rotavirus mortality averted by vaccination in 2016 was heavily concentrated in low-income regions with high child populations, while high-income regions show minimal numbers on this specific metric. The most intense values cluster tightly within the African continent, particularly in the western and central zones.
